ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Cantonese crispy deep-fried egg noodles. Mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for deep-fried Cantonese egg noodles with mixed seafood and meat gravy. A0FHR (Noodles, cooked) used as the closest defensible broader category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Cantonese noodle preparation. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Crispy Egg Noodles as served in a Cantonese restaurant or hawker centre is approximately 500 g, comprising the deep-fried noodle nest and the mixed seafood-and-meat gravy topping.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles, egg, cooked; and mixed seafood/meat composite — HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete Crispy Egg Noodles dish as prepared in Singapore hawker centres. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo entries for cooked egg noodles and mixed seafood/meat dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for egg noodles (FDC ID 20110), cooked pork, prawns, and squid. The deep-frying step increases fat content substantially; the cornstarch-thickened gravy contributes additional carbohydrate. Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of the noodle nest (~40% of dish weight) and the mixed gravy topping (~60% of dish weight). All values are estimates and carry medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg noodles and analogous mixed noodle dishes — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cantonese Crispy Egg Noodles (Sang Mee) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous entry: cooked egg noodles (GI ~40–55, University of Sydney GI Database). Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (low: ≤55; medium: 56–69; high: ≥70). The protein from seafood and meat and the fat from deep-frying moderate the net glycemic impact. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 55.0) / 100 = 30.3 g (based on 500 g serving × 11.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 55.0 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (360 mg per 100 g; ~1800 mg per standard 500 g serving) from seasoned stock, soy sauce, and oyster sauce used in the gravy
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200–250 g, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid consuming the full volume of gravy, as a single standard serving can deliver approximately 1800 mg sodium — approaching the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~30 g GL per standard serving) driven by refined egg noodles and cornstarch-thickened gravy, with a GI of approximately 55 at the upper boundary of the low category
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 200–250 g, pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycemic response, and monitor post-meal blood glucose, as the combination of deep-fried starch and cornstarch gravy can produce a significant glycemic excursion despite the borderline-low GI value.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (3.0 g per 100 g; ~15 g per serving) and trans fat (0.1 g per 100 g) from deep-frying, combined with high sodium and cholesterol (40 mg per 100 g; ~200 mg per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Crispy Egg Noodles as an occasional food only, request steamed or pan-fried noodle alternatives where possible, and limit intake to half a standard serving to reduce saturated fat, trans fat, sodium, and dietary cholesterol exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential from deep-frying in refined vegetable oils and high refined-starch content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption of deep-fried noodle dishes and consider requesting a non-fried noodle alternative such as steamed rice noodles to reduce pro-inflammatory dietary load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Egg Noodles, known in Cantonese as 生面 (Sang Mee or Shēng miàn), is a classic Cantonese noodle dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres, coffee shops, and Cantonese restaurants across Southeast Asia. The dish is defined by its dramatic textural contrast: thin fresh egg noodles are deep-fried in hot oil until they form a rigid, golden-brown, crunchy nest. This crispy noodle base is then topped with a generous ladle of thick, savoury gravy prepared from chicken or pork stock seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil, and thickened with cornstarch. The gravy is finished egg-drop style — beaten egg is swirled in during the final moments of cooking to create silky egg ribbons throughout the sauce. The topping typically includes a combination of sliced pork, pork or chicken liver, prawns, squid, fish cake, and leafy green vegetables such as choy sum. As the diner eats, the hot gravy progressively softens the noodle nest, creating a dynamic eating experience that transitions from crunchy to tender. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g serving provides approximately 750 kcal, 35 g protein, 45 g fat, and 55 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1800 mg per serving) and saturated fat (~15 g per serving), making it a concern for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The GI is approximately 55 (upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney classification) and the glycemic load per serving is high (~30 g GL), reflecting the refined starch from egg noodles and cornstarch gravy. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish, mollusc, fish, egg, or gluten allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy egg noodles?
Can diabetics eat crispy egg noodles?
What is the sodium content of Sang Mee?
What is the difference between Sang Mee and wonton noodles?
Is crispy egg noodles high in fat?
What is the glycemic index of egg noodles?
What toppings are in Sang Mee?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete Crispy Egg Noodles dish as prepared in Singapore. Per-100g values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimates for the component ingredients (egg noodles, mixed seafood, pork, vegetables, cornstarch gravy). The GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach and the variability inherent in hawker-centre preparation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Cantonese noodle dish is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served is not reported in available approved sources. 2. trans_fat_g (0.1 g per 100 g) is retained from the input record; this value reflects trace trans fats from deep-frying in commercial vegetable oils and is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for deep-fried foods. 3. The input record contained two duplicate portion entries for '1 serving (400g)'; only one has been retained. The default serving size has been set to 500 g (1 plate) consistent with typical hawker-centre serving sizes. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (cooked egg noodles); no direct measurement exists for this dish. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (low: ≤55). gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving = (55 × 55.0 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 30.3 g. 5. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork and pork liver. 6. Ontology codes are mapped to broader categories; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for Cantonese crispy deep-fried egg noodles with mixed seafood and meat gravy.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Crispy Egg Noodles', a precise canonical English name. Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are present, satisfying the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (150.0), protein_g (7.0), fat_g (9.0), and carbohydrate_g (11.0) are all present and numeric. nutrient_source documents the HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimation basis. No core macro is zero.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.0), sodium_mg (360.0), saturated_fat_g (3.0), and sugar_g (1.0) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for a deep-fried noodle dish with seasoned gravy.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (55), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (30.3), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (cooked egg noodles), with estimation basis fully documented in gi_source.source_match_notes. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low category (low: ≤55), so gi_category is correctly set to 'low', resolving the Q4 failure from iteration 2.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315595) with honest gap note and correct foodon_match_type 'closest_parent'. FoodEx2 mapped to A0FHR (Noodles, cooked) with honest gap note and correct foodex2_match_type 'closest_parent'. SNOMED CT left null with gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,450 characters). It covers what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutritional profile.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'), and Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains 13 entries covering English variants, Chinese script forms, romanizations, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. aliases_by_language is fully populated and consistent with the flat aliases array. Self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. No discrepancies found.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 hard gate requirements.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with the University of Sydney GI classification scale where GI ≤55 is classified as low. GI value of 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category. Updated gi_source.source_match_notes and notes field to document this classification rationale. Updated type2_diabetes health context key_factor to reflect the corrected gi_category.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: EX000093 Crispy Egg Noodles passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The critical fix in iteration 3 was correcting gi_category from 'medium' to 'low': per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale, a GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (low ≤55; medium 56–69; high ≥70). The previous iteration 2 draft incorrectly assigned 'medium' to GI 55, causing the Q4 failure. All other sections are preserved from iteration 2. The record satisfies the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement (English, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). All core macros are present and non-zero. Secondary nutrients are complete. GI evidence is from the University of Sydney GI Database with full analogous-estimate documentation. The LLM description exceeds 200 characters. All three mandatory health conditions are present with clinical guidance. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent. All -1 sentinel values are documented in notes.
